message.content:
You are an independent blind verifier (verifier B) for a production-hardening pass on the hc-assay repo at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. Determine whether this finding is a TRUE_POSITIVE (a genuine production-readiness defect that should be fixed) or a FALSE_POSITIVE (false, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design, or not a real defect). Be rigorous and skeptical in BOTH directions — do not rubber-stamp, but do not dismiss a real defect on a technicality.

Out-of-scope-by-design exclusions (these are NOT defects): the dataset-agnostic engine receiving opaque inputs; optional import-guarded backends; transcripts intentionally committed (and scrubbed); on-box/loopback data-sovereignty; documented honest-scope limitations.

FINDING K-REL-1 (high): SIGTERM handler swallows process termination when prior disposition is SIG_DFL
Location: src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py ~148-156 (_install_flush_on_exit._handler)
Claim: The installed SIGTERM handler flushes/shuts down the tracer then does `if callable(prior): prior(signum, frame)`. When no prior Python handler exists, prior == signal.SIG_DFL which is NOT callable, so the handler returns WITHOUT terminating — it replaced the default terminate disposition, so the process no longer dies on SIGTERM (docker stop / kill / k8s) until SIGKILL escalation. Tracing is on by default; bootstrap runs on the main thread in run/resume paths. The unit test only asserts flush/shutdown ran, never that termination still happens.

Read the actual code (Read/Grep/Bash) and, where the claim is empirically testable, REPRODUCE it (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ... ; use "python -m pytest" not bare pytest). Judge ONLY K-REL-1. Give your verdict with concrete evidence (what you ran/read, what you observed).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

112	def _warn_if_not_global(provider: Any) -> None:
113	    """If another provider already won the global slot, our manual spans would vanish (#O6)."""

126	def _instrument_langchain() -> None:
127	    """Capture LangChain auto-spans (the bulk reasoning tier uses LangChain) — best-effort (#O5)."""
128	    try:
129	        from openinference.instrumentation.langchain import LangChainInstrumentor
130

132	    except Exception:

136	def _install_flush_on_exit(provider: Any) -> None:
137	    """Flush buffered spans on clean exit (atexit) and on SIGTERM (#O3).
138	
139	    atexit does not run on ``kill``/container-stop, so a main-thread SIGTERM handler also
140	    flushes+shuts down. Both are guarded — tracing teardown must never raise.
141	    """
142	    flush = getattr(provider, "force_flush", None)
143	    if not callable(flush):
144	        return
145	    atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush))
146	    if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread():
147	        try:
148	            prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)
149	
150	            def _handler(signum: int, frame: Any) -> None:
151	                _safe(flush)
152	                _safe(getattr(provider, "shutdown", lambda: None))
153	                if callable(prior):
154	                    prior(signum, frame)
155	
156	            signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)
157	        except (ValueError, OSError):  # pragma: no cover - not on main thread / unsupported
158	            pass
159	
160	
161	def _safe(fn: Any) -> None:
162	    try:
163	        fn()
164	    except Exception:
165	        pass
166	
167	
168	@contextmanager
169	def run_trace_context(run_id: str | None) -> Iterator[None]:
170	    """Stamp ``run_id`` onto every span emitted inside the block (cross-store correlation).
171	
172	    A no-op when ``run_id`` is None or OpenTelemetry is absent.
173	    """
174	    if run_id is None:
175	        yield
176	        return
177	    try:
178	        from opentelemetry import baggage, context
179	    except ImportError:
180	        yield
181	        return
182	    token = context.attach(baggage.set_baggage("assay.run_id", run_id))
183	    try:
184	        yield
185	    finally:
186	        context.detach(token)
187

content.text: The code confirms the structure described in the finding. Now let me verify the claim that `signal.SIG_DFL` is not callable and that replacing the default disposition means the process no longer terminates on SIGTERM.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -c "
import signal
print('SIG_DFL callable?', callable(signal.SIG_DFL))
print('SIG_DFL =', signal.SIG_DFL)
print('default SIGTERM disposition:', signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM))
"
input.description: Check if SIG_DFL is callable and default disposition

content.content:
SIG_DFL callable? False
SIG_DFL = 0
default SIGTERM disposition: 0

content.text: `SIG_DFL` is indeed not callable. Now let me reproduce the actual behavior end-to-end: install a handler mimicking the code, send SIGTERM, and confirm the process does NOT die.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && cat > /tmp/repro_kREL1.py <<'EOF'
import signal, os, time, threading

# Mimic _install_flush_on_exit exactly
flushed = {"flush": False, "shutdown": False}
def flush(): flushed["flush"] = True
def shutdown(): flushed["shutdown"] = True

prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)   # this is SIG_DFL in a fresh process
print("prior =", prior, "callable?", callable(prior))

def _handler(signum, frame):
    flush()
    shutdown()
    if callable(prior):
        prior(signum, frame)

signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)

# Send SIGTERM to ourselves
os.kill(os.getpid(), signal.SIGTERM)
# Give the handler a moment
time.sleep(0.5)
print("STILL ALIVE after SIGTERM. flushed:", flushed)
EOF
python /tmp/repro_kREL1.py; echo "exit code: $?"
input.description: Reproduce: send SIGTERM with SIG_DFL prior and observe survival

content.text: Reproduced. The process survives SIGTERM and exits 0 normally. Let me confirm the contrast: with default disposition (no handler installed) the process dies with signal 143 (128+15).

os.kill(os.getpid(), signal.SIGTERM)
time.sleep(2)
print("SHOULD NOT REACH HERE")

content.text: Confirmed contrast: default disposition terminates (143 = SIGTERM), but the installed handler swallows it (exit 0, "STILL ALIVE"). Now let me verify the operational claims: tracing on by default and bootstrap running on the main thread in run/resume paths.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && grep -rn "_install_flush_on_exit\|def bootstrap\|enabled" src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py | head -40

content.content:
67	def bootstrap_tracing() -> Any:
68	    """Idempotently wire the global OTel tracer provider to the local collector.
69	
70	    Returns the provider, or ``None`` if tracing is disabled, the extra is not installed, or
71	    setup fails — tracing must never be load-bearing, so this degrades gracefully.
72	    """
73	    global _provider
74	    if _disabled():
75	        return None
76	    # Lock the check-then-set so two threads can't both register the global provider.
77	    with _provider_lock:
78	        if _provider is not None:
79	            return _provider
80	        try:
81	            require_loopback_host(TRACING_HOST, what="tracing collector host")
82	            # Bound the OTLP export so a downed collector can never hang teardown (#O3).
83	            os.environ.setdefault("OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_TIMEOUT", "2")
84	            from phoenix.otel import register
85	
86	            provider = register(
87	                project_name=PROJECT_NAME,
88	                endpoint=tracing_endpoint(),
89	                protocol="http/protobuf",  # pin: endpoint is .../v1/traces — never flip to gRPC
90	                set_global_tracer_provider=True,
91	                batch=True,  # async BatchSpanProcessor — never block the run on export (#O2)
92	                verbose=False,  # no stdout banner from a library context
93	            )
94	            _provider = provider
95	            _warn_if_not_global(provider)
96	            _instrument_langchain()
97	            _install_flush_on_exit(provider)
98	            return _provider
99	        except Exception as exc:
100	            # Missing extra / collector down / registration race — run untraced. Graceful
101	            # degradation, but no longer SILENT (pass 3, #F-044): a misconfigured
102	            # ASSAY_TRACING_HOST/PORT or a missing observability extra now leaves a diagnosable
103	            # warning instead of an inexplicably untraced run.
104	            _log.warning(
105	                "assay tracing: setup failed, running untraced (%s: %s)",
106	                type(exc).__name__,
107	                exc,
108	            )
109	            return None
110	
111

content.text: Confirmed: tracing is on by default (only disabled via opt-out `ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING`). Let me verify the run/resume paths in graph.py call bootstrap on the main thread, and confirm the bootstrap actually reaches `_install_flush_on_exit` (i.e., provider has `force_flush`).

100	    trace: bool = True,
101	) -> Any:
102	    """Invoke a compiled graph for ``run_id`` (the checkpointer thread id), within a trace
103	    context, bounded by ``recursion_limit``. The run parks at the first gate ``interrupt``;
104	    resume it with :func:`resume`.
105	
106	    Uses ``durability="sync"``: a governance run must have its parked-at-gate checkpoint written
107	    *before* the operator is notified, so a crash in the (otherwise async) write window cannot
108	    lose the interrupt. (The LangGraph default is ``"async"``.)
109	    """
110	    if trace:
111	        bootstrap_tracing()

117	def resume(
118	    graph: Any,
119	    *,
120	    run_id: str,
121	    gate_id: str,
122	    decision: str,

124	    recursion_limit: int = RECURSION_LIMIT,
125	    trace: bool = True,
126	) -> Any:
127	    """Resume a run parked at a gate with the operator's decision.
128	
129	    ``gate_id`` is the gate the operator intended to decide (from the governance channel) and
130	    is REQUIRED — the gate node's correlation guard rejects it if it does not match the parked
131	    gate, so a stale/misrouted decision cannot resume the wrong gate. Must use the SAME
132	    checkpointer the run started with (``thread_id == run_id``); resume always continues the
133	    latest interrupted state for the thread (no checkpoint_id — see :func:`_thread_config`).
134	    """
135	    _require_langgraph()
136	    from langgraph.types import Command
137	
138	    if trace:
139	        bootstrap_tracing()
140	    config = _thread_config(run_id, recursion_limit)
141	    resume_value = {"gate_id": gate_id, "decision": decision, "rationale": rationale}
142	    with run_trace_context(run_id):
143	        return graph.invoke(Command(resume=resume_value), config=config, durability="sync")
144

content.text:
Both `run` and `resume` call `bootstrap_tracing()` with `trace=True` by default, synchronously on the calling thread (typically main). The Phoenix `register` provider (`TracerProvider`) has `force_flush`, so `_install_flush_on_exit` proceeds to install the SIGTERM handler.

Let me confirm the unit test only asserts flush/shutdown, not termination, as claimed.

input.description: Find tests touching the SIGTERM handler

content.text:
This is conclusive. The test at line 211 explicitly monkeypatches `getsignal` to return `SIG_DFL` (the exact scenario in the finding), then at line 228 calls the handler — and only asserts `calls["shutdown"] >= 1`. It never asserts that the process would terminate. The test would pass even though, in production, the handler swallows SIGTERM.

All claims in the finding are verified:

1. **SIG_DFL not callable** — confirmed (`callable(signal.SIG_DFL)` → False).
2. **Handler swallows termination** — reproduced: process sent SIGTERM stays alive and exits 0, vs. default disposition exiting 143.
3. **Tracing on by default** — `_disabled()` is opt-out only (`ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING`).
4. **Bootstrap on main thread in run/resume** — `graph.run` (line 110-111) and `graph.resume` (line 138-139) both call `bootstrap_tracing()` synchronously with `trace=True` default; `_install_flush_on_exit` installs the handler when `current_thread() is main_thread()`.
5. **Test only asserts flush/shutdown, never termination** — confirmed at test_observability.py:195-229, with `getsignal` monkeypatched to `SIG_DFL`.

This is a genuine production defect: a containerized/k8s deployment would have `docker stop`/`kubectl delete` hang for the full grace period (default 10-30s) before SIGKILL escalation, on every run/resume invocation, because the most common production case is no pre-existing Python SIGTERM handler (prior == SIG_DFL).

The correct fix: when `prior` is `SIG_DFL`, re-raise the default behavior (e.g., reset the handler to `SIG_DFL` and re-send, or `raise SystemExit(143)` / `os._exit`), or handle the `SIG_IGN` case as well.

Not covered by any out-of-scope exclusion (it is not the agnostic engine, an optional backend, transcripts, data-sovereignty, or a documented honest-scope limitation).

input.reasoning:
Verified by reading the code and reproducing the behavior.

CODE: src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:136-158 — _install_flush_on_exit installs a SIGTERM handler that flushes/shuts down the tracer, then only calls the prior handler `if callable(prior)`. There is no else branch.

CLAIM 1 (SIG_DFL not callable): Confirmed empirically — `callable(signal.SIG_DFL)` returns False (SIG_DFL == 0). A fresh process has SIGTERM disposition == SIG_DFL.

CLAIM 2 (handler swallows termination): Reproduced. A script mimicking the handler exactly (prior=getsignal=SIG_DFL, install _handler, os.kill(self, SIGTERM)) printed "STILL ALIVE after SIGTERM" and exited 0. Contrast: an identical script WITHOUT the handler exited 143 (128+SIGTERM). So the installed handler converts the default terminate-on-SIGTERM into a no-op, meaning docker stop/kill/k8s would hang until SIGKILL escalation.

CLAIM 3 (tracing on by default): Confirmed. _disabled() (line 53-54) is pure opt-out via ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING; default is enabled.

CLAIM 4 (bootstrap on main thread in run/resume): Confirmed. orchestration/graph.py run() (110-111) and resume() (138-139) both call bootstrap_tracing() synchronously with trace=True default. bootstrap_tracing -> _install_flush_on_exit(provider) (tracing.py:97); the Phoenix register() provider exposes force_flush, so the SIGTERM handler is installed when running on the main thread.

CLAIM 5 (test never asserts termination): Confirmed. tests/test_observability.py:195-229 (test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm) monkeypatches signal.getsignal to return SIG_DFL (line 211) — the exact vulnerable case — then invokes the handler and only asserts calls["shutdown"] >= 1 (line 229). It never asserts the process would still terminate, so the defect is not caught.

This is a real production-readiness defect (graceful shutdown broken under the most common production case of no pre-existing Python SIGTERM handler) affecting every run/resume invocation. It is not covered by any out-of-scope exclusion. Suggested fix: when prior is SIG_DFL (or SIG_IGN), restore default disposition and re-raise/re-deliver the signal (e.g., signal.signal(SIGTERM, SIG_DFL); os.kill(os.getpid(), SIGTERM)) or raise SystemExit(143) after flushing.

content.text:
Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE.

Finding K-REL-1 is a genuine production defect. The SIGTERM handler at `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:150-156` calls the prior disposition only `if callable(prior)`, with no else branch. When `prior == signal.SIG_DFL` (the common case: no pre-existing Python SIGTERM handler), it is not callable, so after flush/shutdown the handler returns and the process keeps running instead of terminating.

Reproduced empirically: a script mimicking the handler exactly survived SIGTERM and exited 0 ("STILL ALIVE"), while the same script without the handler exited 143. Tracing is on by default (opt-out only), and `graph.run`/`graph.resume` call `bootstrap_tracing()` synchronously on the main thread, so the handler is installed on real run/resume paths. The unit test (`tests/test_observability.py:195-229`) even monkeypatches `getsignal` to `SIG_DFL` but only asserts shutdown ran — never that termination still happens — so it does not catch the regression.

Effect: `docker stop` / `kubectl delete` would hang for the full grace period before SIGKILL escalation on every traced run. Not covered by any out-of-scope exclusion.
